volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: The Two Menaechmuses (Section 5.1.704 - Section 5.2.800)

sections.label: Section 5.1.704
sections.original: Provisam quam mox vir meus redeat domum .
sections.translation: I ’ ll look out to see how soon my husband is going to return home .

sections.label: Section 5.1.705
sections.original: sed eccum video . salva sum , pallam refert .
sections.translation: But here he is ; I see him ; I ’ m all right , he ’ s bringing back the mantle .

sections.label: Section 5.1.706
sections.original: Demiror ubi nunc ambulet Messenio .
sections.translation: I wonder where Messenio can be walking now .

sections.label: Section 5.1.707
sections.original: Adibo atque hominem accipiam quibus dictis meret .
sections.translation: I ’ ll go and receive the fellow with such language as he deserves . ( Accosting him .) Are you not ashamed to come forward in my presence , you disgraceful man , in that garb ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.709b
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.710
sections.original: quae te res agitat , mulier ?
sections.translation: What thing is troubling you , woman ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.710b
sections.original: Etiamne , impudens ,
sections.translation: Do you dare , you shameless fellow , to utter even a single word , or to speak to me ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.712
sections.original: Quid tandem admisi in me , ut loqui non audeam ?
sections.translation: Pray , what wrong have I committed , that I shouldn ’ t dare to speak to you ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.713
sections.original: Rogas me ? o hominis impudentem audaciam !
sections.translation: Do you ask me ? O dear , the impudent audacity of the fellow !

sections.label: Section 5.1.714
sections.original: Non tu scis , mulier , Hecubam quapropter canem
sections.translation: Don ’ t you know , madam , for what reason the Greeks used to say that Hecuba was a bitch [*] ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.715b
sections.original: Non equidem scio .
sections.translation: I don ’ t know , indeed .

sections.label: Section 5.1.716
sections.original: Quia idem faciebat Hecuba quod tu nunc facis :
sections.translation: Because Hecuba used to do the same thing that you are now doing . She used to heap all kinds of imprecations on every one she saw ; and , therefore , for that reason she was properly begun to be called a bitch .

sections.label: Section 5.1.719
sections.original: Non ego istaec tua flagitia possum perpeti .
sections.translation: I can ’ t put up with this disgraceful conduct of yours ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.720
sections.original: nam med aetatem viduam ésse mavelim ,
sections.translation: for I had rather see my life that of a widow , than endure this vile conduct of yours that you are guilty of .

sections.label: Section 5.1.722
sections.original: Quid id ad me , tu te nuptam possis perpeti
sections.translation: What is it to me , whether you are able to endure to live in the married state , or whether you will separate from your husband ? Is it thus the fashion here to tell these stories to a stranger on his arrival ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.725
sections.original: Quas fabulas ? non , inquam , patiar praeterhac ,
sections.translation: What stories ? I say , I ’ ll not endure it henceforth , but live separate rather than put up with these ways .

sections.label: Section 5.1.727
sections.original: Mea quidem hercle causa vidua vivito ,
sections.translation: Troth , so far indeed as I ’ m concerned , do live separate , even so long as Jupiter shall hold his sway .

sections.label: Section 5.1.729
sections.original: At mihi negabas dudum surrupuisse te ,
sections.translation: A little while since you didn ’ t deny that you had purloined it from me ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.730
sections.original: nunc eandem ante oculos attines : non te pudet ?
sections.translation: do you now hold up that same before my eyes ? Are you not ashamed ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.731
sections.original: Eu hercle , mulier , multum et audax et mala es .
sections.translation: O dear ! madam , by my faith , you are both very bold and very perverse . Do you dare to say ( pointing at the mantle ) that this was stolen from you which another woman gave me , for me to get it trimmed ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.734
sections.original: Ne istuc mecastor — iam patrem accersam meum
sections.translation: By heavens , I ’ ll certainly now send for my father ,

sections.label: Section 5.1.735
sections.original: atque ei narrabo tua flagitia quae facis .
sections.translation: and I ’ ll tell him your disgraceful conduct that you are guilty of . Go , Decio ( calling to a SERVANT ) , seek for my father , that he may come along with you to me ; tell him that occasion has arisen for it . I ’ ll . now disclose to him this disgraceful conduct of yours .

sections.label: Section 5.1.738b
sections.original: Sanan es ?
sections.translation: Are you in your senses ? What disgraceful conduct of mine ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.739b
sections.original: Pallam atque aurum meum
sections.translation: When you filch from home my mantle and gold trinkets ,

sections.label: Section 5.1.740
sections.original: domo suppilas tuae uxori et tuae
sections.translation: without the knowledge of your wife , and carry them off to your mistress . Don ’ t I state this correctly ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.742
sections.original: Quaeso hercle , mulier , si scis , monstra quod bibam ,
sections.translation: By my faith , madam , I entreat you , if you know , show me what I ’ m to drink [*] , by means of which I may put up with your impertinence . What person you are taking me to be , I don ’ t know ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.745
sections.original: ego te simitu novi cum Porthaone .
sections.translation: I know you just as well as Parthaon [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.1.746
sections.original: Si me derides , at pol illum non potes ,
sections.translation: If you laugh at me , still , i ’ troth , you can ’ t do so at him ; my father , I mean , who ’ s coming here . Why don ’ t you look back ? Do you know that person ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.748b
sections.original: Novi cum Calcha simul :
sections.translation: Just as well as Calchas [*] do I know him ; I have seen him on that same day on which I have seen yourself before this present day .

sections.label: Section 5.1.750
sections.original: Negas novisse me ? negas patrem meum ?
sections.translation: Do you deny that you know me ? Do you deny that you know my father ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.751
sections.original: Idem hercle dicam , sí avom vis adducere .
sections.translation: Troth , I shall say the same thing , if you choose to bring your grandfather .

sections.label: Section 5.1.752
sections.original: Ecastor pariter hoc atque alias res soles .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you do this and other things just in a like fashion .

sections.label: Section 5.2.753
sections.original: Vt aétas mea ést atque ut hóc usus fácto est
sections.translation: According as my age permits , and as there is occasion to do so , I ’ ll push on my steps and make haste to get along .

sections.label: Section 5.2.755
sections.original: sed id quam mihi facile sit , haud sum falsus .
sections.translation: But how far from easy ’ tis for me , I ’ m not mistaken as to that . For my agility forsakes me , and I am beset with age ; I carry my body weighed down ; my strength has deserted me . How grievous a pack upon one ’ s back is age . For when it comes , it brings very many and very grievous particulars ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.760
sections.original: quas si aútumem omnis , nimis lóngus sermost .
sections.translation: were I now to recount all of which , my speech would be too long . But this matter is a trouble to my mind and heart , what this business can possibly be on account of which my daughter suddenly requires me to come to her ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.763a
sections.original: nec , quíd id sit , mihí certiús facit , quid
sections.translation: and doesn ’ t first let me know what ’ s the matter , what she wants , or why she sends for me . But pretty nearly do I know now what ’ s the matter ;

sections.label: Section 5.2.765
sections.original: credo cum viro litigium natum esse aliquod .
sections.translation: I suspect that some quarrel has arisen with her husband . So are these women wont to do , who , presuming on their portions , and haughty , require their husbands to be obedient to them ; and they as well full oft are not without fault . But still there are bounds , within which a wife ought to be put up with .

sections.label: Section 5.2.770
sections.original: nec pol filia umquam patrem accersit ad se ,
sections.translation: By my troth , my daughter never sends for her father to come to her except when either something has been done wrong , or there is a cause for quarrelling . But whatever it is , I shall now know . And see , I perceive her herself before the house , and her husband in a pensive mood . ’ Tis the same as I suspected .

sections.label: Section 5.2.775
sections.original: áppellabo hanc .
sections.translation: I ’ ll accost her .

sections.label: Section 5.2.775b
sections.original: Ibo advorsum . sálve multum , mí pater .
sections.translation: I ’ ll go and meet him . May every happiness attend you , my father .

sections.label: Section 5.2.776
sections.original: Salva sis . salven advenio ? salven accersi iubes ?
sections.translation: Happiness attend you . Do I find you in good spirits ? Do you bid me be fetched in happy mood ? Why are you sorrowful ? And why does he ( pointing at MENAECHMUS ) in anger stand apart from you ? Something , I know not what , are you two wrangling about [*] between you . Say , in few words , which of the two is in fault : no long speeches , though .

sections.label: Section 5.2.780
sections.original: Nusquam equidem quicquam deliqui : hoc primum te absolvo , pater .
sections.translation: For my part , I ’ ve done nothing wrong ; as to that point do I at once make you easy , father . But I cannot live or remain here on any account ; you must take me away hence immediately .

sections.label: Section 5.2.782b
sections.original: Quid istuc autem est ?
sections.translation: Why , what ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.782c
sections.original: Ludibrio , pater ,
sections.translation: I am made a laughing-stock of , father .

sections.label: Section 5.2.783b
sections.original: Vnde ?
sections.translation: By whom ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.783c
sections.original: Ab illo , quoi me mandavisti , meo viro .
sections.translation: By him to whom you gave me , my husband .

sections.label: Section 5.2.784
sections.original: Ecce autem litigium . quotiens tandem édixi tibi ,
sections.translation: Look at that — a quarrel now . How often , I wonder ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.785
sections.original: ut caveres , neuter ad me iretis cum querimonia ?
sections.translation: have I told you to be cautious , that neither should be coming to me with your complaints .

sections.label: Section 5.2.786
sections.original: Qui ego istuc , mi pater , cavere possum ?
sections.translation: How , my father , can I possibly guard against that ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.786b
sections.original: Men interrogas ?
sections.translation: Do you ask me ? ⋯

sections.label: Section 5.2.787
sections.original: Nisi non vis .
sections.translation: unless you don ’ t wish .

sections.label: Section 5.2.787b
sections.original: Quotiens monstravi tibi , viro ut morem geras ,
sections.translation: How often have I told you to be compliant to your husband . Don ’ t be watching what he does , where he goes , or what matter he ’ s about .

sections.label: Section 5.2.790
sections.original: At enim ille hinc amat meretricem ex proxumo .
sections.translation: Why , but he ’ s in love with a courtesan here close by .

sections.label: Section 5.2.790b
sections.original: Sane sapit ,
sections.translation: He is exceedingly wise : and for this painstaking of yours , I would even have him love her the more .

sections.label: Section 5.2.792
sections.original: Atque ibi potat .
sections.translation: He drinks there , too .

sections.label: Section 5.2.792b
sections.original: Tua quidem ille causa potabit minus ,
sections.translation: And will he really drink the less for you , whether it shall please him to do so there or anywhere else ? Plague on it , what assurance is this ? On the same principle , you would wish to hinder him from engaging to dine out ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.795
sections.original: neve quemquam accipiat alienum apud se . serviren tibi
sections.translation: or from receiving any other person at his own house . Do you want husbands to be your servants ? You might as well expect , on the same principle , to be giving him out his task , and bidding him sit among the female servants and card wool .

sections.label: Section 5.2.798
sections.original: Non equidem mihi te advocatum , pater , adduxi , sed viro .
sections.translation: Why , surely , father , I ’ ve sent for you not to be my advocate , but my husband ’ s : on this side you stand [*] , on the other you plead the cause .

sections.label: Section 5.2.799b
sections.original: Si ille quid deliquerit ,
sections.translation: If he has done wrong in anything ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.800
sections.original: multo tanto illum accusabo , quam te accusavi , amplius .
sections.translation: so much the more shall I censure him than I ’ ve censured you . Since he keeps you provided for and well clothed , and finds you amply in female servants and provisions , ’ tis better , madam , to entertain kindly feelings .
